Exploring housing demand

2026-07-20

In this memo:

  • Descriptive statistics about DC’s current & historic population
  • Population extrapolations to 2030
  • Next steps

DC’s historical population

Until 1950, DC’s population grew steadily each decade. The expansion of the federal government during the Civil War and both World Wars led to population growth spurts. (The biggest decade-on-decade growth came during the 1850s and 60s, as the American bureaucracy was born, and formerly enslaved people fled neighboring southern states).

After 1950, the District’s population sank, as tens of thousands of mostly-white residents relocated to the suburbs.

Starting around 2000, this decades-long downward trend reversed, although the COVID pandemic knocked back DC’s population growth again, at least temporarily.


Extrapolations to 2030

The chart below shows several potential future population trends:

  • The highest-growth scenario: Janeese Lewis-George’s 72,000 housing unit goal achieved by 2030, and ~all of those units are filled.
  • A high-growth scenario: Population grows at average annual rate observed during 2012-2020 period.
  • A trend growth scenario: Population increases linearly after 2023-2024.
  • A pessimistic scenario: Population declines at 0.1%/year.

The chart below shows the estimated number of each type of housing unit that are associated with each scenario.



Filtering estimate

  • As of the 2018-2022 5-year ACS, there were ~14,700 DC renter households between 50% and 100% of AMI who were rent-burdened (paying more than 30% of their income in rent).
  • Mast (2023) finds that constructing 100 new market rate units leads 45-70 households in cheaper units to leave those units and start renting in the new units. This opens up those 45-70 cheaper units for more moderate-income people to rent.
  • To create moving chains that would allow DC households at 50-100% AMI to move to cheaper units, you might need to build 21,000–33,000 new market-rate units.
  • Renters below 50% AMI wouldn’t benefit as much, especially in the short run, because their incomes are low enough that more market-rate housing doesn’t necessarily create moving-chain opportunities for them.

Household size and dwelling unit statistics

The charts below show DC’s demographic and housing characteristics in the 2024 5-year ACS, weighted by individual person. Group quarters residents are not shown (their inclusion doesn’t change the charts much).

  • A plurality of DC households are comprised of one person.
  • Most DC residents rent.
  • A plurality of DC residents live in row houses and duplexes.




One-person households have grown slightly as a share of total households over time. Larger households have shrunk as a share of total households. Perhaps unsurprisingly, studios as a share of total units have grown over time; large households are also now more likely to live in smaller units (charts in the appendix).


Existing research

COG (2019): ~36,000 units needed to house the projected number of workers the COG expects the region will “need” in 2030.

Here’s their math:

  • COG says the DMV will have 3.774 million jobs by 2030.
  • COG says that the ideal “jobs per household ratio” is 1.54.
  • \(\frac{3,774,000 jobs}{1.54 jobs / household} =\) 2,450,000 households “needed”
  • COG says that the DMV will have ~2.375 million households by 2030 under their baseline scenario.
  • 2,450,000 - 2,375,000 = 75,000 household / housing unit shortfall.
  • COG says there are 2,130,000 housing units now; to reach the 2,450,000 target, the DMV needs to build a total of 320,000 housing units.
  • DC’s share of that is roughly 36,000 units (this is partly where Mayor Bowser’s 36k housing unit goal came from).

The people at COG are smarter than me, so maybe this estimate is spot on, but it feels a little dicey to project housing need in a decade like this. It’s also sensitive to your assumptions: If you change the target jobs/households ratio to 1.5, the shortfall doubles; change the ratio to 1.6 and the method would suggest we have a housing surplus. Same goes for COG’s jobs & housing projections.

Maybe they just needed a number they could all agree on. In which case: makes sense! But projecting the number of jobs, housing, and housing demand 10 years from now feels a little quixotic. Many of these estimates feel this way. I understand that COG is a transportation planning organization, so they may need some rough estimate of these trends for their own purposes.

Final note: the COG isn’t maximizing welfare or productivity in this paper. They’re not estimating how many people would like to live in the DMV, if given the opportunity. They’re estimating the number of houses needed in the DMV to reduce in-commuting from surrounding counties.

Freddie Mac (2020): DC ~34,000 units short

AEI (2025): 32,149 units short

  • AEI basically takes an estimate of the national housing unit shortage (6 million homes, in the higher half of these national estimates, many of which hover around 4 million)
  • And they allocate out shortages to each state based on that state’s \(\frac{home price}{local income}\) ratio. All else equal, this will make DC’s housing shortage look less severe, because DC, while having high rents, also has very high median income (even if many people in absolute terms live on very modest incomes in the District).
  • This gives them a housing shortage in DC of ~34,000 units.

Up For Growth (2025): ~15,000 units short

Kevin Corinth & Hugo Dante (2022): ~170,000 units short

These authors estimate how many units would be built in the “absence of supply constraints and the actual number of homes.” In other words, how many homes people would build, before DC rents started to come in line with rents in alternative places to live, and the price incentive to build more homes petered out because the market reached equilibrium. This is closer to what economists hear when someone says “there’s a housing shortage.”

Moodys (2025): This project estimates housing shortages by Census tract. I’m trying to get access.

Next steps

The main challenge with estimating housing demand is that we don’t directly observe demand. We see how many people choose to live in DC, and what they pay to rent or buy a home. But those decisions are influenced by the amount of housing available.

In other words, we don’t see this complete picture:

We only see a series of equilibrium points, where those two curves meet, over time.

To trace out the demand curve, we really need an exogenous shock that shifts supply, but does not influence demand:

These kinds of shocks are hard to come by. It’s not clear that DC has had any discrete jumps in housing supply during a period in which there were not confounding changes in demand.

Next steps:

  • Clarify specific research questions.
  • Use the 2016 reduction in parking minimums to estimate housing demand.
  • Replicate this Brookings paper that uses the Kitagawa–Oaxaca–Blinder decomposition to estimate a national housing supply shortage, but for DC specifically.
